news_summary: Recent scientific advances include breakthroughs in targeting harmful bacteria, innovative cancer detection technology, and novel methods to prevent rabies, while new research highlights the impact of paternal tobacco use on children's metabolism.
news_summary_fr: Parmi les avancées scientifiques récentes, on peut citer des percées dans la lutte contre les bactéries nocives, des technologies innovantes de dépistage du cancer et de nouvelles méthodes de prévention de la rage, tandis que de nouvelles recherches mettent en évidence l'impact du tabagisme paternel sur le métabolisme des enfants.
news_summary_es: Entre los avances científicos recientes se incluyen avances decisivos en la lucha contra las bacterias nocivas, tecnologías innovadoras para la detección del cáncer y nuevos métodos para prevenir la rabia, mientras que nuevas investigaciones ponen de relieve el impacto del consumo de tabaco por parte del padre en el metabolismo de los niños.

articles.title: MIT scientists discover gut protein that traps and kills dangerous bacteria
articles.summary: Scientists at MIT have discovered that a little-known protein called intelectin-2 plays a powerful double role in defending the gut. The protein strengthens the mucus layer that lines the gastrointestinal tract while also trapping and disabling harmful bacteria that try to break through. By binding to sugars on both mucus molecules and bacterial surfaces, intelectin-2 forms a protective barrier and can even destroy microbes, including some that resist antibiotics.
articles.content:
The moist surfaces that line the body contain specialized molecules that help defend against microbes and prevent infections and inflammation. Among these protective molecules are lectins, a group of proteins that identify microbes and other cells by attaching to sugars on their surfaces.

Researchers at MIT have now identified one lectin with particularly strong antimicrobial effects against bacteria living in the gastrointestinal (GI) tract. The protein, called intelectin-2, attaches to sugar molecules on bacterial membranes. This interaction traps the bacteria and slows their growth. The protein can also link together components of mucus, reinforcing the mucus layer that protects the gut lining.

"What's remarkable is that intelectin-2 operates in two complementary ways. It helps stabilize the mucus layer, and if that barrier is compromised, it can directly neutralize or restrain bacteria that begin to escape," says Laura Kiessling, the Novartis Professor of Chemistry at MIT and the senior author of the study.

Because of its broad antimicrobial activity, intelectin-2 may have potential as a therapeutic tool, the researchers say. It could also help reinforce the mucus barrier in people with conditions such as inflammatory bowel disease.

Amanda Dugan, a former MIT research scientist, and Deepsing Syangtan PhD '24 are the lead authors of the study, which appears in Nature Communications.

A Multifunctional Immune Protein

Evidence suggests that the human genome encodes more than 200 lectins, which are carbohydrate-binding proteins involved in immune defense and communication between cells. Kiessling's lab has been studying how lectins interact with carbohydrates and recently focused on a group known as intelectins. In humans, this family includes two proteins, intelectin-1 and intelectin-2.

Although these two lectins share a similar structure, intelectin-1 has a unique feature. It binds only to carbohydrates found on bacteria and other microbes. About a decade ago, Kiessling and her colleagues determined the structure of intelectin-1, but its precise biological functions are still not fully understood.

At that time, researchers suspected that intelectin-2 might also contribute to immune defense, although there was limited experimental evidence. Dugan, who was then a postdoctoral researcher in Kiessling's lab, began investigating the role of intelectin-2 in more detail.

In humans, intelectin-2 is consistently produced by Paneth cells in the small intestine. In mice, however, the protein appears to be produced by mucus-secreting Goblet cells in response to inflammation or certain parasitic infections.

How Intelectin-2 Strengthens the Gut Barrier

The researchers discovered that intelectin-2 from both humans and mice can bind to a sugar molecule called galactose. This sugar is commonly found in mucins, the molecules that form mucus. When intelectin-2 attaches to these mucins, it links them together and strengthens the mucus barrier that protects the intestinal lining.

Galactose also appears in carbohydrates displayed on the surface of some bacterial cells. The team showed that intelectin-2 can attach to microbes carrying these sugars, including several pathogens known to cause gastrointestinal infections.

Over time, the trapped microbes begin to break apart, suggesting that intelectin-2 disrupts their cell membranes and ultimately kills them. This antimicrobial effect works against many different bacteria, including some that are resistant to conventional antibiotics.

The researchers believe these two functions help shield the GI tract from infection.

"Intelectin-2 first reinforces the mucus barrier itself, and then if that barrier is breached, it can control the bacteria and restrict their growth," Kiessling says.

Potential for Treating Gut Diseases and Resistant Bacteria

In people with inflammatory bowel disease, levels of intelectin-2 can become either unusually low or unusually high. Reduced levels may weaken the mucus barrier, while excessive amounts could eliminate beneficial bacteria that normally inhabit the gut. The researchers suggest that therapies designed to restore balanced levels of intelectin-2 could help these patients.

"Our findings show just how critical it is to stabilize the mucus barrier. Looking ahead, we can imagine exploiting lectin properties to design proteins that actively reinforce that protective layer," Kiessling says.

Intelectin-2 can also neutralize or eliminate pathogens such as Staphylococcus aureus and Klebsiella pneumoniae, which are often difficult to treat with antibiotics. Because of this ability, the protein may one day be developed into a new antimicrobial treatment.

"Harnessing human lectins as tools to combat antimicrobial resistance opens up a fundamentally new strategy that draws on our own innate immune defenses," Kiessling says. "Taking advantage of proteins that the body already uses to protect itself against pathogens is compelling and a direction that we are pursuing."

The research was funded by the National Institutes of Health Glycoscience Common Fund, the National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Disease, the National Institute of General Medical Sciences, and the National Science Foundation.

Other contributors to the study include Charles Bevins, a professor of medical microbiology and immunology at the University of California at Davis School of Medicine; Ramnik Xavier, a professor of medicine at Harvard Medical School and the Broad Institute of MIT and Harvard; and Katharina Ribbeck, the Andrew and Erna Viterbi Professor of Biological Engineering at MIT.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of a gut protein, intelectin-2, that strengthens the mucus barrier and kills harmful bacteria, including antibiotic-resistant strains. This breakthrough has broad implications for improving gut health, treating inflammatory bowel disease, and combating antimicrobial resistance, benefiting a wide population. The article provides detailed context on the protein's function, potential therapeutic applications, and the research background, fulfilling criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: MIT scientists find gut protein that blocks and kills harmful bacteria
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Our gut is lined with mucus that protects us from harmful bacteria. Scientists have been studying proteins called lectins that help defend the body by sticking to sugars on microbes. One protein, intelectin-2, was not well understood until now.

**What happened** – Researchers at MIT discovered that intelectin-2 plays two important roles in protecting the gut. It strengthens the mucus barrier by linking mucus molecules together, and it also traps harmful bacteria by attaching to sugars on their surfaces. This protein can even destroy bacteria, including some that are hard to kill with antibiotics. The study was led by Laura Kiessling and published recently in Nature Communications.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because intelectin-2 both reinforces the body's natural defenses and actively fights bacteria that try to break through. It works against a wide range of bacteria, including dangerous ones that resist antibiotics. This could help people with gut diseases like inflammatory bowel disease, where the mucus barrier is weak or damaged.

**What's next step** – Scientists hope to develop new treatments that use intelectin-2 or similar proteins to strengthen the gut’s mucus barrier and fight infections. They also want to explore how balancing intelectin-2 levels could improve health for people with gut problems and antibiotic-resistant infections.

**One-sentence takeaway** – MIT researchers found that the protein intelectin-2 protects the gut by strengthening mucus and killing harmful bacteria, offering new ways to treat gut diseases and infections.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques du MIT découvrent une protéine intestinale qui bloque et tue les bactéries dangereuses
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Notre intestin est protégé par une couche de mucus qui empêche les bactéries nuisibles d’entrer. Des scientifiques étudient des protéines appelées lectines qui aident le corps en se fixant sur les sucres des microbes. Une protéine, l’intelectine-2, était peu connue jusqu’à présent.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs du MIT ont découvert que l’intelectine-2 joue deux rôles clés pour protéger l’intestin. Elle renforce la couche de mucus en reliant ses molécules, et elle piège aussi les bactéries dangereuses en s’attachant aux sucres présents sur leur surface. Cette protéine peut même détruire des bactéries, y compris certaines résistantes aux antibiotiques. L’étude, dirigée par Laura Kiessling, a été publiée dans Nature Communications.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car l’intelectine-2 renforce la défense naturelle du corps et combat activement les bactéries qui tentent de passer. Elle agit contre de nombreuses bactéries, y compris des pathogènes difficiles à éliminer. Cela pourrait aider les personnes atteintes de maladies intestinales comme la maladie inflammatoire de l’intestin, où la barrière de mucus est affaiblie.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques souhaitent développer de nouveaux traitements utilisant l’intelectine-2 ou des protéines similaires pour renforcer la barrière de mucus et lutter contre les infections. Ils veulent aussi comprendre comment équilibrer les niveaux d’intelectine-2 pour améliorer la santé des patients avec des problèmes intestinaux et des infections résistantes.

**Une phrase clé** – Des chercheurs du MIT ont découvert que la protéine intelectine-2 protège l’intestin en renforçant le mucus et en tuant les bactéries nuisibles, ouvrant la voie à de nouveaux traitements.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos del MIT descubren proteína intestinal que bloquea y mata bacterias peligrosas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Nuestro intestino está protegido por una capa de moco que impide la entrada de bacterias dañinas. Los científicos estudian proteínas llamadas lectinas que ayudan al cuerpo al adherirse a los azúcares en los microbios. Una proteína llamada intelectina-2 era poco conocida hasta ahora.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores del MIT descubrieron que la intelectina-2 cumple dos funciones importantes para proteger el intestino. Fortalece la barrera de moco al unir sus moléculas y también atrapa bacterias dañinas al unirse a los azúcares en su superficie. Esta proteína incluso puede destruir bacterias, incluidas algunas resistentes a los antibióticos. El estudio fue dirigido por Laura Kiessling y publicado en Nature Communications.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque la intelectina-2 refuerza la defensa natural del cuerpo y combate activamente las bacterias que intentan penetrar. Funciona contra muchas bacterias, incluidas algunas difíciles de eliminar. Esto podría ayudar a personas con enfermedades intestinales como la enfermedad inflamatoria intestinal, donde la barrera de moco está debilitada.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos esperan desarrollar nuevos tratamientos que usen intelectina-2 o proteínas similares para fortalecer la barrera de moco y combatir infecciones. También quieren investigar cómo equilibrar los niveles de intelectina-2 para mejorar la salud de quienes tienen problemas intestinales o infecciones resistentes.

**Una frase clave** – Investigadores del MIT encontraron que la proteína intelectina-2 protege el intestino al fortalecer el moco y matar bacterias dañinas, ofreciendo nuevas formas de tratar enfermedades intestinales e infecciones.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a glowing, protective mucus layer inside a stylized gut tunnel, with intricately linked sugar-like chains forming a strong barrier, while gentle, abstract protein shapes resembling elegant nets trap and dissolve small, simplified bacterial forms, all rendered in soft natural tones of cream, pale green, and muted gold.

articles.title: Scientists create a cancer flashlight that lights up tumors
articles.summary: Researchers have developed a tiny antibody that can find a common cancer protein and make tumors light up during PET scans. In tests with mice, tumors containing the protein EphA2 glowed clearly when the antibody was used. This could help doctors quickly identify patients who may benefit from new targeted cancer treatments. The method may also provide a faster and less invasive alternative to traditional testing.
articles.content:
Researchers at the University of Missouri are developing a new way to determine which cancer patients are most likely to benefit from targeted therapies by illuminating tumors in medical scans.

Barry Edwards, an associate professor of biochemistry in the School of Medicine, recently designed a very small antibody that seeks out EphA2, a protein frequently present in cancer tumors. After creating the antibody, he attached a radioactive marker that makes the molecule visible during a positron emission tomography (PET) scan.

Antibody "Flashlight" Lights Up Cancer Tumors

In experiments using mice, Edwards showed that this cancer detecting "flashlight" clearly illuminated tumors that produced EphA2. The results suggest that tagging the antibody could help doctors detect cancers that contain this protein and determine which patients might respond to therapies designed to target EphA2-positive tumor cells while leaving healthy tissue unharmed.

"By finding out which patients have high or low amounts of EphA2, we can determine who is most likely to benefit from a targeted cancer treatment," Edwards, who also has an appointment in the College of Arts and Science, said. "There is no sense in giving a treatment that won't work to a patient, so this new process we created saves time and money while advancing precision medicine."

Faster and Less Invasive Than Traditional Methods

Doctors currently depend on biopsies and MRI scans to evaluate tumors in cancer patients. These methods can be invasive, require significant time, and often provide limited insight into the specific proteins found within cancer cells. Edwards, who uses advanced imaging technology at Mizzou's Molecular Imaging and Theranostics Center for his research, hopes to advance this technique from preclinical studies to human clinical trials within the next seven years.

"This new targeted approach is noninvasive, and you can get results from the imaging in hours rather than days, which can be huge for patients traveling long distances to seek treatment," Edwards said. "By making the process easier and faster for both patients and clinicians, we're showing that precision medicine is a win-win."

The study, titled "Preclinical evaluation of anti-EphA2 minibody-based immunoPET agent as a diagnostic tool for cancer," was published in Molecular Imaging and Biology.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant medical breakthrough where researchers developed a novel antibody that can noninvasively illuminate tumors expressing a common cancer protein, EphA2, during PET scans. This advancement promises faster, less invasive, and more precise cancer diagnosis and treatment selection, potentially benefiting many cancer patients by improving targeted therapy outcomes and reducing unnecessary treatments. The story is focused, detailed, and has broad implications for cancer care.
articles.personality_title: New antibody ‘flashlight’ helps doctors see cancer tumors more clearly
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists are looking for better ways to find out which cancer treatments will work best for patients. Current methods like biopsies can be slow and uncomfortable.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Missouri created a tiny antibody that attaches to a common cancer protein called EphA2. They added a safe radioactive marker to this antibody so it lights up tumors during PET scans. In tests on mice, tumors with EphA2 glowed clearly, helping doctors see them more easily.

**Impact** – This discovery means doctors might quickly identify patients who have tumors with EphA2 and could benefit from special targeted treatments. It is faster and less invasive than current methods, which often involve surgery or long waits for results.

**What's next step** – The research team plans to move from animal tests to human clinical trials in about seven years. If successful, this technique could change how doctors diagnose and treat certain cancers.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new antibody that lights up cancer tumors during scans could speed up diagnosis and help doctors choose better treatments for patients.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouvel anticorps « lampe de poche » aide les médecins à mieux voir les tumeurs cancéreuses
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent de meilleures méthodes pour savoir quels traitements contre le cancer fonctionnent le mieux. Les méthodes actuelles comme les biopsies peuvent être lentes et inconfortables.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université du Missouri ont créé un tout petit anticorps qui se fixe à une protéine courante du cancer appelée EphA2. Ils ont ajouté un marqueur radioactif sûr à cet anticorps pour qu’il éclaire les tumeurs lors des scanners PET. Lors d’essais sur des souris, les tumeurs avec EphA2 brillaient clairement, aidant les médecins à mieux les voir.

**Impact** – Cette découverte permettrait aux médecins d’identifier rapidement les patients dont les tumeurs ont EphA2 et qui pourraient bénéficier de traitements ciblés spécifiques. C’est plus rapide et moins invasif que les méthodes actuelles, souvent chirurgicales ou longues.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe de recherche prévoit de passer des tests sur animaux aux essais cliniques humains d’ici environ sept ans. Si cela fonctionne, cette technique pourrait changer la façon dont les médecins diagnostiquent et traitent certains cancers.

**Une phrase clé** – Un nouvel anticorps qui éclaire les tumeurs cancéreuses lors des scanners pourrait accélérer le diagnostic et aider les médecins à choisir de meilleurs traitements.
articles.personality_title_es: Un nuevo anticuerpo ‘linterna’ ayuda a los médicos a ver mejor los tumores cancerosos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos buscan mejores formas de saber qué tratamientos contra el cáncer funcionan mejor. Los métodos actuales como las biopsias pueden ser lentos y molestos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Missouri crearon un anticuerpo muy pequeño que se une a una proteína común del cáncer llamada EphA2. Le añadieron un marcador radiactivo seguro para que ilumine los tumores durante las exploraciones PET. En pruebas con ratones, los tumores con EphA2 brillaron claramente, ayudando a los médicos a verlos mejor.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento podría permitir a los médicos identificar rápidamente a los pacientes con tumores que tienen EphA2 y que podrían beneficiarse de tratamientos dirigidos especiales. Es más rápido y menos invasivo que los métodos actuales, que suelen ser quirúrgicos o tardan mucho.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo de investigación planea pasar de pruebas en animales a ensayos clínicos en humanos en unos siete años. Si tiene éxito, esta técnica podría cambiar cómo los médicos diagnostican y tratan ciertos cánceres.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Un nuevo anticuerpo que ilumina tumores durante las exploraciones podría acelerar el diagnóstico y ayudar a los médicos a elegir mejores tratamientos para los pacientes.
articles.image_prompt: A softly glowing, intricately detailed antibody shaped like a delicate key gently illuminating a cluster of abstract, warmly colored tumor-like shapes, set against a calm, neutral background, symbolizing precise and hopeful medical detection.

articles.title: Daily briefing: Vaccine-carrying mosquitoes could inoculate bats against rabies
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 12 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00825-0New technique aims to prevent bats from spreading disease, the science behind health supplements and the ‘same-ifying’ effect of writing with AI.
articles.content:
Hello Nature readers, would you like to get this Briefing in your inbox free every day? Sign up here.

Fruit bats carry Nipah virus, which has infected people in India and several other countries.Credit: C. K Thanseer/DeFodi images via Getty

Aedes aegypti mosquitoes engineered to carry vaccines in their saliva have been used in the lab to inoculate bats against the rabies and Nipah viruses. Researchers fed the mosquitoes blood that contained a vaccine against one of the two viruses, which were then passed on either when the insects fed on bats or when the bats ate them. The team hopes that this method could be used to stop such viruses from spilling over’ from bats into humans. But other researchers are sceptical that the method would be effective in the wild.

Nature | 4 min read

Reference: Science Advances paper

People who use large language models are picking up writing patterns, reasoning methods and even opinions from the chatbots, some research suggests. This pattern threatens to homogenize human writing and discourse, argue some computer scientists, and could even influence text written by people that aren’t first-hand AI users. But not all researchers in the field agree. In one study, a team of scientists found that certain groups of writers held on to their personal writing style after using AI, and some even developed one that was more markedly distinct from that of the LLM.

Nature | 6 min read

Reference: arXiv preprint 1 (not peer reviewed), Science Advances paper, Trends in Cognitive Science opinion piece & arXiv preprint 2 (not peer reviewed)

After missiles damaged oil depots and refineries this week, Iran’s capital Tehran has been blanketed by pollutant-laden ‘black rain’. Experts say that this rain probably contains both soot, which can damage people’s lungs and eyes, and cancer-causing chemicals such as benzene and toluene, released by the burning of oil-refining byproducts. The pollutants could be dispersed in the air if there are no new fires, says atmospheric chemist Gabriel da Silva. But Tehran’s position in the Alborz mountain range can lead to temperature inversions, a meteorological phenomenon that traps polluted air masses. Rain could also disperse the chemicals, but that could lead to contamination in soil and waterways, da Silva says.

Nature | 5 min read

A species of gut bacteria that proliferates as mice get older plays a part in cognitive decline. Researchers found that the bacterium, Parabacteroides goldsteinii, interferes with signalling along sensory nerves that connect the gut to the brain by producing fatty acids, which trigger the release of inflammatory molecules from immune cells. Although the experiments were conducted in mice, the gut–brain circuit that the team identified “is likely conserved in humans”, says biochemist David Vauzour, which, if confirmed, could lead to gut-targeted therapies to reverse cognitive decline in people.

Nature | 5 min read

Reference: Nature paper

Features & opinion

The United Kingdom is looking to make big changes to how it funds research — and physics is in the crosshairs. The country’s largest research funder, UK Research and Innovation, is ending or reducing investment in projects in particle physics, astronomy and nuclear physics. Some of the biggest cuts will hit UK physicists’ participation in international projects such as CERN, Europe’s particle-physics laboratory. Nuclear physicist Paul Howarth, the new leader of the country’s Institute of Physics, tells Nature that “constructive dialogue” with the government is needed, or the country risks losing the next generation of scientists.

Nature | 5 min read

By some estimates, some 60% of US and European adults take dietary supplements regularly — whether stalwarts such as omega-3 or up-and-comers such as collagen and lion’s mane. But hard evidence for the effectiveness of some supposedly ‘evidence-backed’ supplements can be difficult to come by. Compared with research on vitamins and minerals that treat nutrient deficiencies, “the science is rather more sketchy” on compounds thought to be beneficial to health, says nutritional scientist Paul Coates, who formerly led the arm of the US National Institutes of Health responsible for supplement research. “It’s done by good people, often in model systems, but the effect sizes are pretty tiny.”

Nature | 14 min read

This article is part of Nature Spotlight: Nutrition
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a novel and potentially impactful scientific breakthrough where genetically engineered mosquitoes are used to vaccinate bats against dangerous viruses like rabies and Nipah. This innovative method could prevent viral spillover from bats to humans, addressing a significant public health risk with broad societal implications. The article provides sufficient detail about the technique, its goals, and the scientific context, making it a substantive and inspiring development in disease prevention.
articles.personality_title: Engineered mosquitoes used to vaccinate bats against deadly viruses
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Bats can carry dangerous viruses like rabies and Nipah, which sometimes spread to humans and cause serious illness. Stopping these viruses before they jump from bats to people is a big challenge for scientists.

**What happened** – Researchers have created mosquitoes that carry vaccines in their saliva. In a lab, these mosquitoes were fed blood containing vaccines against rabies or Nipah virus. When the mosquitoes bit bats or when bats ate the mosquitoes, the bats received the vaccines. This new method helped protect bats from these viruses.

**Impact** – This approach is unique because it uses mosquitoes as tiny vaccine carriers to stop viruses in bats before they can infect humans. If it works outside the lab, it could reduce outbreaks of deadly diseases. However, some scientists are unsure if it will be as effective in the wild, where conditions are harder to control.

**What's next step** – Researchers will need to test this vaccination method in natural bat populations to see if it truly prevents virus spread. They will also study any possible effects on the environment and other animals before it can be used widely.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have developed a new way to vaccinate bats using specially designed mosquitoes, aiming to stop dangerous viruses from spreading to people.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des moustiques modifiés vaccinant les chauves-souris contre des virus mortels
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les chauves-souris peuvent porter des virus dangereux comme la rage et le virus Nipah, qui peuvent parfois infecter les humains et causer de graves maladies. Empêcher ces virus de passer des chauves-souris aux humains est un grand défi pour les scientifiques.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs ont créé des moustiques portant des vaccins dans leur salive. En laboratoire, ces moustiques ont été nourris avec du sang contenant des vaccins contre la rage ou le virus Nipah. Quand les moustiques ont piqué les chauves-souris ou quand les chauves-souris ont mangé les moustiques, elles ont reçu le vaccin. Cette nouvelle méthode a aidé à protéger les chauves-souris contre ces virus.

**Impact** – Cette approche est unique car elle utilise des moustiques comme petits transporteurs de vaccins pour arrêter les virus chez les chauves-souris avant qu’ils n’infectent les humains. Si elle fonctionne en milieu naturel, elle pourrait réduire les épidémies de maladies mortelles. Cependant, certains scientifiques doutent de son efficacité hors laboratoire, où les conditions sont plus difficiles à contrôler.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs devront tester cette méthode de vaccination sur des populations naturelles de chauves-souris pour voir si elle empêche vraiment la propagation des virus. Ils étudieront aussi les effets possibles sur l’environnement et les autres animaux avant une utilisation plus large.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont développé un nouveau moyen de vacciner les chauves-souris grâce à des moustiques spécialement conçus, dans le but d’empêcher la transmission de virus dangereux aux humains.
articles.personality_title_es: Mosquitos modificados usados para vacunar murciélagos contra virus mortales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los murciélagos pueden portar virus peligrosos como la rabia y el virus Nipah, que a veces infectan a humanos y causan enfermedades graves. Evitar que estos virus pasen de murciélagos a personas es un gran reto para los científicos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores crearon mosquitos que llevan vacunas en su saliva. En laboratorio, alimentaron a estos mosquitos con sangre que contenía vacunas contra la rabia o el virus Nipah. Cuando los mosquitos picaron a los murciélagos o cuando los murciélagos se comieron a los mosquitos, recibieron la vacuna. Este método nuevo ayudó a proteger a los murciélagos de estos virus.

**Impacto** – Este enfoque es único porque usa mosquitos como pequeños portadores de vacunas para detener los virus en murciélagos antes de que infecten a humanos. Si funciona fuera del laboratorio, podría reducir brotes de enfermedades mortales. Sin embargo, algunos científicos dudan que sea igual de efectivo en la naturaleza, donde las condiciones son difíciles de controlar.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores deberán probar este método de vacunación en poblaciones naturales de murciélagos para ver si realmente previene la propagación del virus. También estudiarán posibles efectos en el medio ambiente y otros animales antes de usarlo ampliamente.

**Frase clave** – Científicos han desarrollado una nueva forma de vacunar murciélagos usando mosquitos especialmente diseñados, con el objetivo de detener la propagación de virus peligrosos a las personas.
articles.image_prompt: A gentle scene of a group of fruit bats resting peacefully in a softly lit cave, with delicate Aedes aegypti mosquitoes hovering nearby, their translucent wings catching warm light as faint, glowing vaccine droplets shimmer on their proboscises, symbolizing the transmission of protection against viruses.

articles.title: Fathers’ tobacco use linked to metabolic changes in their children
articles.summary: A new study in mice suggests that a father’s nicotine exposure could influence the metabolic health of his children. Researchers found that when male mice consumed nicotine, their offspring showed changes in how their bodies handled sugar, including differences in insulin and glucose levels and altered liver function. These shifts may be linked to a higher risk of diabetes and related metabolic diseases.
articles.content:
New research published in the Journal of the Endocrine Society suggests that a father's nicotine exposure could influence how his offspring process sugar. In a mouse study, scientists found that paternal nicotine intake led to metabolic changes in the next generation. These alterations may affect how the body handles glucose and could increase the risk of developing diabetes.

Diabetes is already a widespread health issue in the United States. According to the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, an estimated 40.1 million Americans are living with the condition. People with diabetes face a higher chance of developing complications such as heart disease, kidney disease and nerve damage. Because diabetes affects more than 12 percent of Americans and is a lifelong condition, treatment and management create a substantial financial burden.

The study also notes that tobacco use remains one of the most significant preventable causes of poor health outcomes. Reducing behaviors such as smoking and electronic cigarette use could help slow the growing diabetes epidemic. This may be especially important for men, who use tobacco products more frequently than women.

Researchers Identify Metabolic Changes in Offspring

"When male mice consumed nicotine in their drinking water, their offspring had metabolic alterations that appear to impact the way the body metabolizes sugar," said the study's senior author, Raquel Chamorro-Garcia, Ph.D., of the University of California, Santa Cruz, in Santa Cruz, Calif. "This suggests that tobacco use in men is linked with an increased risk of their descendants developing diabetes."

To investigate this link, researchers tracked the offspring of male mice that had been given nicotine in their drinking water. These offspring were compared with those from a control group whose fathers had not been exposed to nicotine.

The results showed clear metabolic differences between the groups. Female offspring of nicotine exposed fathers had lower insulin levels and lower fasting glucose levels compared with the control group. Male offspring also showed reduced blood glucose levels and changes in liver function. Conditions such as obesity and diabetes are known contributors to metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease.

Importance of Male Health Before Conception

"Considering the evidence that male exposure can increase the likelihood of their children developing chronic diseases, it is crucial to incorporate male health into preconception care," said Chamorro-Garcia, assistant professor of microbiology and environmental toxicology at UC Santa Cruz. "Our findings suggest fathers' use of tobacco products may have lasting effects on their children's health."

Because the mice were exposed only to pure nicotine during the experiment, the researchers were able to isolate its effects. According to Chamorro-Garcia, the findings indicate that metabolic changes observed in the offspring were not caused by other byproducts found in cigarettes or additives used in e-cigarettes.

Other study authors are Stephanie Aguiar, Truman Natividad, Daniel Davis, and Carlos Diaz-Castillo, all at UC Santa Cruz.

Funding for the research came from the National Institutes of Health's National Institute of Environmental Health Sciences, the University of California Office of the President Tobacco-related Disease Research Program award, and University of California, Santa Cruz Start-up Funds.
articles.reasoning: The study reveals a significant health insight linking paternal nicotine exposure to metabolic changes in offspring, highlighting the importance of male health before conception. This finding has broad implications for public health and diabetes prevention, offering a tangible pathway to reduce disease risk through behavior change.
articles.personality_title: Father’s nicotine use affects children’s metabolism, study finds
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long studied how a mother’s health affects children, but new research shows a father’s habits can also influence his offspring’s health. Tobacco use, especially nicotine, is common among men and linked to many health problems.

**What happened** – A study at the University of California, Santa Cruz, gave male mice nicotine in their drinking water. Their children were then tested and showed changes in how their bodies managed sugar, including differences in insulin and glucose levels, and altered liver function. These changes could increase the risk of diabetes.

**Impact** – This research is important because it shows that a father’s nicotine use before conception can affect his children’s metabolism. It highlights a new way tobacco use can harm health, not just for the user but for future generations. Understanding this can help guide better health advice for men planning to become fathers.

**What’s next step** – Researchers hope to study if similar effects happen in humans and explore ways to reduce these risks. This could lead to new health programs focused on men’s habits before having children.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A father’s nicotine use may change how his children’s bodies process sugar, increasing their risk of diabetes and related diseases.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’usage de nicotine chez le père influence le métabolisme de ses enfants, selon une étude
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques ont longtemps étudié comment la santé de la mère affecte les enfants, mais une nouvelle recherche montre que les habitudes du père peuvent aussi influencer la santé de ses descendants. L’usage de nicotine est courant chez les hommes et lié à plusieurs problèmes de santé.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une étude à l’Université de Californie à Santa Cruz a donné de la nicotine à des souris mâles par leur eau de boisson. Leurs enfants ont ensuite montré des changements dans la façon dont leur corps gère le sucre, avec des différences dans les niveaux d’insuline et de glucose, ainsi qu’un fonctionnement modifié du foie. Ces changements pourraient augmenter le risque de diabète.

**Impact** – Cette recherche est importante car elle montre que l’usage de nicotine par un père avant la conception peut affecter le métabolisme de ses enfants. Elle révèle une nouvelle façon dont le tabac peut nuire à la santé, non seulement pour l’utilisateur mais aussi pour les générations futures. Cela peut aider à mieux conseiller les hommes qui prévoient de devenir pères.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs souhaitent vérifier si ces effets sont aussi présents chez l’homme et trouver des moyens de réduire ces risques. Cela pourrait mener à de nouveaux programmes de santé ciblant les habitudes des hommes avant la paternité.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L’usage de nicotine par un père peut modifier la manière dont ses enfants traitent le sucre, augmentant leur risque de diabète et de maladies associées.
articles.personality_title_es: El uso de nicotina en padres afecta el metabolismo de sus hijos, revela estudio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante mucho tiempo, los científicos han estudiado cómo la salud de la madre afecta a los hijos, pero una nueva investigación muestra que los hábitos del padre también pueden influir en la salud de sus descendientes. El uso de nicotina es común en hombres y está relacionado con varios problemas de salud.

**Qué pasó** – Un estudio en la Universidad de California, Santa Cruz, dio nicotina a ratones machos en su agua. Sus crías mostraron cambios en la forma en que sus cuerpos manejan el azúcar, incluyendo diferencias en los niveles de insulina y glucosa, y cambios en la función del hígado. Estos cambios podrían aumentar el riesgo de diabetes.

**Impacto** – Esta investigación es importante porque muestra que el uso de nicotina por parte del padre antes de la concepción puede afectar el metabolismo de sus hijos. Revela una nueva forma en que el tabaco puede dañar la salud, no solo del usuario, sino también de futuras generaciones. Esto puede ayudar a dar mejores consejos a hombres que planean ser padres.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores esperan estudiar si efectos similares ocurren en humanos y buscar maneras de reducir estos riesgos. Esto podría llevar a nuevos programas de salud enfocados en los hábitos de los hombres antes de tener hijos.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El uso de nicotina por parte del padre puede cambiar cómo sus hijos procesan el azúcar, aumentando su riesgo de diabetes y enfermedades relacionadas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a protective adult mouse gently holding a small glowing sugar crystal above a nest of tiny mice, with soft, natural earth tones highlighting subtle, flowing patterns symbolizing metabolic pathways connecting the father mouse to its offspring.
